text: Your own Self-Realization is the greatest service you can render the world.
context_full: This statement appears paradoxical to people who have been taught that service means active outward help. Ramana's teaching is rooted in the Advaita understanding that the individual self and the world are not separate — they appear within the one Self, like waves in an ocean. If the 'I' that sees itself as a separate helper is itself a construction, then the most fundamental service is to see through that construction. A person who has realized the Self radiates something more fundamental than good works: the actual demonstration, in one living human being, that liberation is possible and real.
reflection_prompt: What would you most want to contribute to the world? And is there a way in which the quality of your own inner state — your clarity, your freedom from anxiety, your capacity to be present — is actually the most important factor in whether that contribution is genuine?

text: The question 'Who am I?' is not really meant to get an answer; the question is meant to dissolve the questioner.
context_full: Ramana's central practice is self-inquiry (atma vichara) — the investigation of the sense of 'I'. Not as an intellectual exercise seeking a clever answer, but as a direct pointing of attention back to the one who is aware. When you ask 'Who am I?' sincerely and follow the question inward, the conceptual answers — 'I am my name, my body, my history, my thoughts' — are seen as objects of awareness rather than as awareness itself. The investigation continues until the questioner itself is no longer found as a separate entity. What remains is pure awareness — not a blank nothing, but the luminous ground of all experience.
reflection_prompt: Set aside five minutes. Sit quietly. Ask: who is aware right now? Not what you are aware of — the sounds, the sensations, the thoughts — but who is doing the being aware. Don't try to answer with words. Simply turn attention back toward its own source and stay there as long as you can.

text: Silence is never-ending speech. Your noisy discussions are a patch of quiet compared to the eternal sound of silence.
context_full: Ramana spent years in states of profound silence, communicating primarily through presence. His teaching on silence (mauna) is paradoxical: genuine silence is not the absence of noise but the presence of something prior to both noise and quiet — the unconditioned awareness in which all sounds appear and disappear. This is the 'speech' that never ends: the teaching that is conveyed not through words but through the direct transmission of the quality of the teacher's own realization. Words can point; silence can transmit.
reflection_prompt: When did you last experience genuine inner silence — not the absence of noise but the presence of something spacious and still underneath all your thoughts and concerns? What were the conditions that allowed it? How could you create even brief access to that quality today?

text: Happiness is your nature. It is not wrong to desire it. What is wrong is seeking it outside when it is inside.
context_full: This is the Advaita teaching on the nature of happiness in its most direct form. The ordinary assumption is that happiness is a state to be achieved by obtaining certain conditions — pleasure, success, love, security. But Ramana points out that we have all had moments of happiness in which we were not aware of any external cause. The happiness that arises on the resolution of a desire is not from the object obtained — it is from the temporary cessation of seeking, the brief rest in one's own nature. The Self is ananda — bliss — by nature. The error is the persistent projection outward.
reflection_prompt: Recall a moment when you were genuinely happy — not because of any specific thing, just a moment of deep okayness or peace. Where was that happiness coming from? Was it in the object or circumstance, or was it something in your own awareness that was briefly free from seeking?

text: Mind is consciousness which has put on limitations. You are originally unlimited and perfect. Later you take on limitations and become the mind.

The world is so unhappy because it is ignorant of the true Self. Man's real nature is happiness. Happiness is inborn in the true Self. Man's search for happiness is an unconscious search for his true Self.
The degree of freedom from unwanted thoughts and the degree of concentration on a single thought are the measures to gauge spiritual progress.
context_full: This is Ramana's practical criterion for measuring inner development — not experiences of light or bliss or visions, not correct doctrine or ritual observance, but something that can be directly observed: how much of the mind is taken up by unwanted, compulsive thoughts, and how easily and completely can it gather itself around one object? These are the two faces of the same development: a mind that is not scattered is a mind that can be concentrated. A mind that can be concentrated is one in which the Self begins to become visible.
reflection_prompt: On an ordinary day, what proportion of your mental activity is compulsive — thoughts you didn't choose and don't want, but can't stop? And how easily can you bring your full attention to bear on one thing for ten minutes without interruption? These are the measures. What do they tell you?

text: Surrender is giving oneself up to the original cause of one's being.
If you hold this feeling of 'I' long enough and closely enough, you find that it takes you to a place from which you don't want to return.
source: Talks with Sri Ramana Maharshi (composite, widely attributed)
